g.data-narration: Welcome to Deadlock Characterization - a critical concept in Operating Systems that helps identify and prevent system deadlocks.
A deadlock occurs when processes block each other, each holding resources another needs, creating a circular wait with no way forward.
g.text: A state where processes are blocked because each holds a resource
another needs, forming a circular wait that prevents any progress.

g.data-narration: Four conditions must hold simultaneously for a deadlock: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait.
g.text: All four must hold simultaneously for deadlock to occur

g.data-narration: First, mutual exclusion: at least one resource must be non-sharable - only one process can use it at any given time.
g.text: At least one resource must be held in non-sharable mode.
Only ONE process can use the resource at a time.
If another requests, it must wait until released.

g.data-narration: Second, hold and wait: a process must hold at least one resource while waiting for additional resources held by others.
g.text: Process must hold at least ONE resource while waiting for
additional resources held by OTHER processes.

g.data-narration: Third, no preemption: resources cannot be forcibly taken away. A process must voluntarily release resources after completing its task.
g.text: Resources cannot be preempted - cannot be forcibly removed.
Process must voluntarily release resources after completion.

g.data-narration: Fourth, circular wait: a set of processes must form a circular chain where each waits for a resource held by the next.
g.text: A set of waiting processes: P0→P1→P2→...→Pn→P0
Each process waits for a resource held by the next.

g.data-narration: A Resource Allocation Graph visualizes system state with processes as circles and resources as rectangles with instance dots.
g.text: Visual tool to model and analyze system resource allocation
Processes: Circles (P1, P2, P3...) | Resources: Rectangles with instance dots

g.data-narration: Request edges go from process to resource (requesting), while assignment edges go from resource to process (allocated).
If the RAG has NO cycles, no deadlock. With cycles: single instance resources guarantee deadlock, multi-instance needs more analysis.
g.text: Cycle is necessary but NOT sufficient

g.data-narration: In this example: P1 holds R1 and wants R2, P2 holds R2 and wants R1. The cycle P1→R2→P2→R1→P1 confirms deadlock.
g.text: P1 holds R1, requests R2 | P2 holds R2, requests R1

g.data-narration: Three strategies: Prevention ensures a condition cannot hold, Avoidance uses algorithms like Banker's, Detection allows deadlocks then recovers.
For exams: memorize all four conditions, practice RAG drawing, distinguish between prevention/avoidance/detection, and always check for cycles first.
g.text: 1. Memorize all four conditions with examples - know which condition each example violates
2. Draw and analyze RAGs - cycle + single instance = guaranteed deadlock
3. Differentiate handling methods: Prevention (restrict), Avoidance (check), Detection (allow + recover)

g.data-narration: In summary: four conditions cause deadlocks, RAGs help visualize and detect them, and systems use prevention, avoidance, or detection strategies.
g.text: Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
